mapping.a_element: scattering problem for the sine-Gordon equation with discrete eigenvalues on the unit circle
mapping.b_element: semiclassical limit of the focusing nonlinear Schrödinger equation with a finite-gap ansatz for the complex phase function

mapping.a_element: Krein stability theory for symplectic matrices and definite Krein signature of eigenvalues
mapping.b_element: variational principle and critical point theory for the complex phase function

mapping.a_element: counting and locating discrete eigenvalues for a non-self-adjoint scattering problem
mapping.b_element: explicit strong asymptotics for solutions via a scalar complex phase function

mapping.a_element: isospectral flow of the sine-Gordon equation
mapping.b_element: semiclassical soliton ensemble as a sequence of exact solutions

brief_justification: While both papers address spectral properties of integrable systems, Paper A focuses on eigenvalue location and counting for a non-self-adjoint scattering problem using Krein stability theory, whereas Paper B employs a steepest descent method for Riemann-Hilbert problems and a finite-gap ansatz to derive semiclassical asymptotics. The underlying machinery—symplectic spectral theory vs. variational and critical point methods—differs fundamentally.
